title: 🎙️ Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling' — how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Most genuine arts/culture/media journalism should land well above 0, even without a Cariboo tie-in:
- 80-100: Cariboo/Williams Lake-specific arts and culture news — community arts council grants or murals, local festivals, Indigenous film/storytelling, community theatre, museum/heritage programs, a Cariboo author or artist receiving an award, community radio or local publishing.
- 55-79: Rural BC or Canadian arts/culture journalism without a specific Cariboo tie — CBC Arts features on Indigenous storytelling, regional festivals, community media projects, heritage preservation, creative-economy stories in small towns, digital storytelling tools and platforms.
- 30-54: General arts, culture, media, or entertainment journalism with real creative/storytelling substance but no rural or community angle (national awards shows, mainstream film/TV/music industry coverage, publishing industry news).
- 15-29: Tangentially creative content (a tech feature that touches on media/publishing/streaming) or local Cariboo civic news with no arts/culture angle.
- 0-14: No arts, culture, media, or storytelling dimension at all.

Strong fits include: community arts journalism (arts councils, local festivals, heritage programs), oral history and cultural preservation projects, Indigenous film and storytelling, community theatre and local media, rural arts funding and the creative economy in small towns, and digital storytelling platforms and tools. Also strong fits: community media tech (podcasting, streaming, local publishing), creative tech economy, open-source arts tools, Cariboo arts festivals using digital outreach, animation/film/media production in rural BC, local radio/podcast infrastructure, and how rural communities use technology for creative expression. A story about a Cariboo community mural, a CBC Arts feature on BC Indigenous storytelling, a rural arts council receiving grants, or a Cariboo author/artist shortlisted for an award is a strong fit even without an explicit technology angle — cultural journalism matters to this audience. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): medical and health research with no cultural/storytelling angle; working lands and resource economy stories with no cultural framing; wildlife and nature stories without creative or community media context; data centre infrastructure and AI investment news; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime coverage; financial market and stock movement stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Williams Lake Tribune] Christmas in July Food Drive supports Williams Lake Salvation Army
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

## Christmas in July Food Drive supports Williams Lake Salvation Army Published 6:38 pm Wednesday, July 22, 2026 **By rebeccadyok** The Williams Lake Salvation Army received donations, including a $1,036 cheque, from the Williams Lake Fire Department's annual Christmas in July Food Drive on Wednesday, July 22\. (Photo submitted) In just a few hours, dozens of non-perishable food items and $1,036 in community donations were collected by the Williams Lake Fire Department to support the Salvation Army. Approximately 35 firefighters canvassed neighbourhoods on Tuesday, July 21, from 6 to 9 p.m.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Fuel Tank Orca Mural in Palmer Station, Antarctica
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

The fuel tank mural at Palmer Station is one of the most recognizable landmarks at this remote Antarctic research base. Painted on the station’s large fuel tank, the mural features a leaping orca and adds a unique piece of public art to the otherwise industrial scenery. Visible from the water as ships approach the station, it has become a familiar symbol of Palmer Station for residents and visitors alike.

The mural stands out against the surrounding Antarctic landscape and serves as a reminder that even in one of the world’s most isolated workplaces, people bring creativity and personality to their surroundings.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Germany told parents to destroy this talking doll
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

In 2014, a talking doll named My Friend Cayla was voted Innovative Toy of the Year. Three years later the German government was telling parents to destroy it. Cayla listened to a child through a hidden microphone, beamed the audio over Bluetooth to a phone app, searched the internet for an answer, and spoke back after about a one-second delay. — Read the rest

The post Germany told parents to destroy this talking doll appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists revive a powerful antibiotic that superbugs had defeated
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The combination restored the antibiotic’s ability to kill drug-resistant E. faecium, raising hopes that similar chemical helpers could rescue other failing medicines.
items.summary: Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The
items._excerpt: Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The combination restored the antibiotic’s ability to kill drug-resistant E. faecium, raising hopes that similar chemical helpers could rescue other failing medicines.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Ike Towell’s Grave in Columbus, Texas
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Isaac "Ike" Towell was born on November 25, 1849 and died, by suicide, on February 22, 1934 at the age of 83 in Columbus, Texas.  

Ike was the city marshal of Columbus in 1885 and a lifelong "infidel".  He was known as a man of unwavering principals and had many friends near and far.  An outspoken atheist, he was recognized for his uncompromising honesty, his opposition to alcohol and tobacco, and his fearless, courageous nature. 

About a year before his death, he arranged for a headstone to be placed at his future gravesite.  A few days before he passed away, he purchased his own coffin and left detailed instructions regarding his funeral arrangements.  The service consisted of a final message written by Mr. Towell and was directed to be read by someone "not afraid that lightening would strike during the service".

Over 100 people were present at his service to hear the following message:

"Especially do I want to impress the fact upon the people that one child was born, passed through the years of youth, grew to manhood, lived a tolerably respectable life, reached old age, died and could be buried without the assistance of the clergy."

"It is my will and I so direct that my funeral be the cheapest possible. I do not hold with carrying a person from county to county for the sole purpose of burial.

"My religion consists of doing right and loving justice. I affirm that all men should tell the truth and pay their debts. I do not believe in any God, devil, ghost or savior and I have opposed tobacco, whiskey, gambling, lying and stealing practically all my life. If any one of the clergy ever calls my name after death I insist that he speak the truth about me, a thing I have never known a preacher to do about a dead disbeliever. The clergy have never been able to bribe me with the promise of a beautiful home in a fictitious heaven nor bluff my by their everlasting punishment in a hell of fire and brimstone."

Headstone Inscription:

"HERE RESTS
IKE TOWELL
AN INFIDEL WHO HAD
NO HOPE OF HEAVEN
NOR FEAR OF HELL
WAS FREE OF SUPERSTITION
TO DO RIGHT AND LOVE
JUSTICE WAS HIS RELIGION"
items.summary: Isaac "Ike" Towell was born on November 25, 1849 and died, by suicide, on February 22, 1934 at the age of 83 in Columbus, Texas. Ike was the city marshal of Columbus in 1885 and a lifelong "infidel". He was known as a man of unwavering principals and had many friends near and far. An outspoken

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] La Casa del Serpentello in Vignale Monferrato, Italy
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

What makes the Serpentello House truly special is its unique artistic character. The façade and many of its decorations are entirely covered with handcrafted ceramic tiles, each one unique and personally designed and created by the owner, Luciano Serpentello, in 1925.

This remarkable attention to detail transforms the house into a true work of art, giving it a distinctive and unforgettable atmosphere.

Non v'è arte né simmetria,
Unico pregio la fantasia.
In pochi anni di mia vita
Da me quest'opera venne compita.
Se non bella è originale
Ed è difficile trovar l'eguale.
Un contadino vi ha posto mano,
Tal Serpentello signor Luciano.

Translated by Google:

No art or symmetry is found here,
Its only merit is imagination.
In but a few years of my life,
I brought this work to completion.
If not beautiful, it is original,
And its equal is hard to find.
A farmer set his hand to it—
Signor Luciano, known as "Serpentello."

As the inscription above suggests, the Serpentello House is often regarded as a "visual encyclopedia" of rural life and the boundless imagination of its creator.

This remarkable attention to

items.title: [Kottke.org] Brian Eno on the Cherished Sounds of Failure
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Near the end of 1995, the artist and producer Brian Eno wrote the following about new media in his diary, which entries were later collected in a book called A Year with Swollen Appendices.

Whatever you now find weird, ugly, uncomfortable and nasty about a new medium will surely become its signature. CD distortion, the jitteriness of digital video, the crap sound of 8-bit — all these will be cherished and emulated as soon as they can be avoided.

It’s the sound of failure: so much of modern art is the sound of things going out of control, of a medium pushing to its limits and breaking apart. The distorted guitar is the sound of something too loud for the medium supposed to carry it. The blues singer with the cracked voice is the sound of an emotional cry too powerful for the throat that releases it. The excitement of grainy film, of bleached-out black and white, is the excitement of witnessing events too momentous for the medium assigned to record them.

Note to the artist: when the medium fails conspicuously, and especially if it fails in new ways, the listener believes something is happening beyond its limits.

Nailed it.

Tags: Brian Eno
items.summary: Near the end of 1995, the artist and producer Brian Eno wrote the following about new media in his diary, which entries were later collected in a book called A Year with Swollen Appendices . Whatever you now find weird, ugly, uncomfortable and nasty about a new medium will surely become its
items._excerpt: Near the end of 1995, the artist and producer Brian Eno wrote the following about new media in his diary, which entries were later collected in a book called A Year with Swollen Appendices . Whatever you now find weird, ugly, uncomfortable and nasty about a new medium will surely become its signature. CD distortion, the jitteriness of digital video, the crap sound of 8-bit — all these will be cherished and emulated as soon as they can be avoided. It’s the sound of failure: so much of modern art is the sound of things going out of control, of a medium pushing to its limits and breaking apart.

items.title: [Animikii News River] "AI moves fast. Human hearts move slow."
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

News River Express

News River Express is back with three articles worth your time, plus a bit of our own news.

We built this summer format so we can spend more time outside with our plants and our people, and still get the best links to your inbox.

This week is the topic everyone is talking about: AI, and where it meets Indigenous Data Sovereignty.

How Indigenous technologists are building data sovereignty into AI

BetaKit

Our CEO and Co-Founder Jeff Ward spoke at Mila's Indigenous AI Gathering in Montréal. The line that stayed with him came from Canada's Minister of AI and Digital Innovation, Evan Solomon:

"AI moves fast. Human hearts move slow."

The Minister came to an Indigenous-led space to present and to listen, weeks after Canada's national AI strategy launched. That's where trust starts, and it's how we build at Animikii: technology made with love starts with human hearts and lets love set the pace.

The BetaKit piece covers some of the conversations. Participants in the Indigenous Pathfinders in AI program pitched tools that keep data inside the community network. Diné geneticist Krystal Tsosie put the stakes plainly:

"[Data] determines what the system sees, what it optimizes for, who benefits, and who is harmed. Our community's data is already on this pathway, and the question is whether or not our community governs what it becomes."

Data centers are booming. Indigenous leaders want help protecting their lands.

Grist

AI infrastructure is hungry for land, energy, and water, and Indigenous delegates took that to the UN's Expert Mechanism on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples. A single hyperscale data centre can draw as much electricity as 100,000 households. Delegates called for free, prior, and informed consent, and some US tribes have passed outright moratoriums. As Sámi Parliament member Maren Storslett said:

"We must not only ask what AI can do, but what it should do."

Respecting First Nations knowledge on Wikimedia platforms

Wikimedia Australia

Wikimedia Australia and the Indigenous-owned firm Terri Janke and Company have released a guide for handling Indigenous knowledge across Wikipedia, Commons, and Wikidata. It matters for AI because Wikimedia trains many large language models, so errors and colonial framing scale with them. The guide uses a risk-assessment approach built on one question: who has the cultural authority to tell this story? As Terri Janke and Company's Shevaun Wright puts it:

"It's not about content moderation, but about content integrity."

And in Animikii news: we are testing ethical AI with an Indigenous community

We've partnered with CEIMIA on a first practical use case for ethical AI and data governance with an Indigenous community in Canada. It pairs two tools: our Niiwin platform, and the Data Roadmap CEIMIA built for the Global Partnership on AI.

Most talk about responsible AI stays at the level of principles. This pilot asks what those principles look like when a community runs them on its own data, with tools it controls. Niiwin carries OCAP®, CARE, and UNDRIP principles into how the system behaves. Testing CEIMIA's roadmap on Indigenous data should push the tool somewhere more inclusive, because community data raises questions government data never does.

It's a small, concrete pilot to help answer a bigger question: can these systems advance Indigenous rights, not just avoid harming them? That's what we're setting out to test. More information on that pilot here.

Want to talk it through? Book an AI conversation and Niiwin demo direct with our CEO Jeff: grab a time.

This week is the topic everyone is talking about: AI, and where it meets Indigenous Data Sovereignty. How Indigenous technologists are building data sovereignty into AI BetaKit Our CEO and Co-Founder Jeff Ward spoke at Mila's Indigenous AI Gathering in Montréal. The line that stayed with him came from Canada's Minister of AI and Digital Innovation,

items.title: [The Marginalian] How Not to Waste Your Life
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

“Let me not seem to have lived in vain,” the Danish astronomer Tycho Brahe whispered on his deathbed, not realizing that the astronomical tables he was leaving behind would become the portal through which Kepler arrives at the laws of planetary motion; not realizing that the measure of an unwasted life is not what outlives it but how it was lived — how much integrity and authenticity and creative vitality filled these numbered days, these unrepeatable hours. Most of us will not leave behind a revolutionary insight into the nature of the universe, but we too forget that no matter… read article

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Ancient ferns turned Triassic Europe into a wildfire inferno
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Ancient fern savannahs may have transformed parts of Europe into a wildfire-prone inferno during the end-Triassic mass extinction. New fossil evidence suggests the ferns repeatedly regrew after fires, creating fresh fuel that helped prolong the devastation.

items.title: [The Marginalian] Borges on How to Conquer Time
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

"Time is the substance I am made of. Time is a river which sweeps me along, but I am the river; it is a tiger which destroys me, but I am the tiger; it is a fire which consumes me, but I am the fire."
items.summary: "Time is the substance I am made of. Time is a river which sweeps me along, but I am the river; it is a tiger which destroys me, but I am the tiger; it is a fire which consumes me, but I am the fire."
